Property lawyer David Smith of Bishop & Sewell has questioned whether the proposed changes will achieve their stated goals. Whilst the government suggests reducing average transaction times from 17 to 13 weeks represents progress, Smith notes this remains far slower than systems in countries like Norway where completions typically take just four weeks.

The consultation documents acknowledge similarities with the ill-fated Home Information Packs (HIPs), introduced in 2007 before being scrapped in 2010. Smith observes: "The government's claim of 'slashing delays' seems optimistic when comparing the proposed 13-week timeframe with international best practice."

Sales packs raise familiar concerns

The proposed mandatory sales packs have drawn particular scrutiny, with critics highlighting several potential issues:

  • Unclear content requirements - the government has yet to specify what information must be included
  • Potential cost burdens - compiling packs may increase expenses for sellers and estate agents
  • Limited impact - buyers typically rely on conveyancers rather than reviewing documentation directly

Smith cautions that without changes to how buyers engage with property information or reforms to the legal framework, sales packs may prove as ineffective as their HIP predecessors. The consultation reportedly admits the parallels between the two approaches, raising questions about whether lessons from the HIPs experiment have been properly considered.

As the proposals develop, industry observers suggest homeowners and prospective buyers might wish to monitor how these reforms could affect transaction times and costs. With the property market facing multiple challenges, the effectiveness of these measures may become clearer when more detailed plans emerge.

item.title: Hobson’s choice: Stamp duty or Burnham’s mansion tax? Which root canal treatment would you prefer, sir?
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Stamp Duty vs Mansion Tax: Comparing Two Property Tax Approaches

As political discussions around property taxation intensify, homeowners may wish to consider the potential implications of two competing approaches: the existing Stamp Duty Land Tax (SDLT) system versus proposed alternatives like an annual mansion tax. Both models aim to generate government revenue from property transactions or ownership, but they operate in fundamentally different ways.

SDLT currently represents a significant source of Treasury income, generating £9-11 billion annually through charges applied when purchasing property. The graduated system introduced in 2014 sees rates climb progressively, reaching effective rates of 19% for overseas buyers purchasing additional properties. While reliable for government coffers, critics argue this transactional tax creates market distortions by discouraging mobility and trapping capital in existing properties.

The Economic Impact of Property Taxation

The current SDLT system has drawn criticism for potentially stifling economic activity in several ways:

  • Reducing housing market fluidity by making moves between properties more expensive
  • Locking homeowners into properties that may no longer suit their needs
  • Diverting funds that could otherwise circulate through consumer spending on home improvements or services

Proponents of alternative approaches like an annual wealth tax suggest such models could spread the tax burden more evenly while avoiding the market stagnation associated with large upfront transaction costs. However, the practical implementation of such systems raises its own set of challenges around valuation methodologies and collection mechanisms.

Looking Ahead: Potential Reforms

With housing policy likely to remain a key political battleground, borrowers might consider how different taxation models could affect their property decisions. While SDLT represents a known quantity after nearly a decade of operation, any shift toward annual property taxes would require careful evaluation of:

  • Long-term cost comparisons between the two systems
  • Potential impacts on property values in different price brackets
  • The administrative burden on homeowners and tax authorities

As the debate continues, homeowners may find it prudent to stay informed about potential changes to property taxation that could influence their financial planning. The choice between these different approaches ultimately reflects broader questions about how society balances revenue generation with housing market functionality.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Stamp Duty vs Mansion Tax: Comparing Two Property Tax Approaches

As political discussions around property taxation intensify, homeowners may wish to consider the potential implications of two competing approaches: the existing Stamp Duty Land Tax (SDLT) system versus proposed alternatives like an annual mansion tax. Both models aim to generate government revenue from property transactions or ownership, but they operate in fundamentally different ways.

SDLT currently represents a significant source of Treasury income, generating £9-11 billion annually through charges applied when purchasing property. The graduated system introduced in 2014 sees rates climb progressively, reaching effective rates of 19% for overseas buyers purchasing additional properties. While reliable for government coffers, critics argue this transactional tax creates market distortions by discouraging mobility and trapping capital in existing properties.

The Economic Impact of Property Taxation

The current SDLT system has drawn criticism for potentially stifling economic activity in several ways:

  • Reducing housing market fluidity by making moves between properties more expensive
  • Locking homeowners into properties that may no longer suit their needs
  • Diverting funds that could otherwise circulate through consumer spending on home improvements or services

Proponents of alternative approaches like an annual wealth tax suggest such models could spread the tax burden more evenly while avoiding the market stagnation associated with large upfront transaction costs. However, the practical implementation of such systems raises its own set of challenges around valuation methodologies and collection mechanisms.

Looking Ahead: Potential Reforms

With housing policy likely to remain a key political battleground, borrowers might consider how different taxation models could affect their property decisions. While SDLT represents a known quantity after nearly a decade of operation, any shift toward annual property taxes would require careful evaluation of:

  • Long-term cost comparisons between the two systems
  • Potential impacts on property values in different price brackets
  • The administrative burden on homeowners and tax authorities

As the debate continues, homeowners may find it prudent to stay informed about potential changes to property taxation that could influence their financial planning. The choice between these different approaches ultimately reflects broader questions about how society balances revenue generation with housing market functionality.

item.title: New platform aims to cut traditional estate agents out of the property sales process
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

New Online Platform Aims to Revolutionise Property Sales

A Leicestershire-based startup is preparing to launch an innovative digital platform that could change how homeowners sell properties in England and Wales. YooSell, launching on 24 June, offers a self-service alternative to traditional estate agents, allowing sellers to create listings, upload photographs and communicate directly with potential buyers through an online dashboard.

Technology Transforming the Property Market

The property sector continues to experience significant digital disruption, with platforms like YooSell joining a growing trend of tech solutions challenging conventional sales methods. The company suggests its platform may particularly appeal to:

  • Cost-conscious sellers looking to reduce estate agency fees
  • Tech-savvy homeowners comfortable managing financial transactions online
  • Sellers wanting greater control over their property listing and viewings

Founder Balram Kumar commented: "With living costs rising, our goal is to help homeowners retain more of their property's value. While technology has transformed most financial transactions, many still feel constrained by traditional property sales methods."

The Future of Property Sales?

YooSell's leadership team draws parallels between their platform and successful online marketplaces in other sectors. Chief Operating Officer Daniel McNeill noted: "We aim to become the AutoTrader equivalent for property. Millions already buy and sell vehicles online comfortably - we believe homeowners deserve similar convenience and transparency when making one of life's most significant financial decisions."

The launch comes as recent industry reports suggest increasing consumer appetite for digital alternatives in property transactions, particularly among younger homeowners. While traditional estate agents still dominate the market, platforms offering greater seller control and reduced fees continue gaining traction.

As the property market evolves, homeowners might wish to research all available options when considering their next move. Digital platforms could potentially offer cost savings, though sellers may need to weigh this against handling more of the sales process themselves.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

New Online Platform Aims to Revolutionise Property Sales

A Leicestershire-based startup is preparing to launch an innovative digital platform that could change how homeowners sell properties in England and Wales. YooSell, launching on 24 June, offers a self-service alternative to traditional estate agents, allowing sellers to create listings, upload photographs and communicate directly with potential buyers through an online dashboard.

Technology Transforming the Property Market

The property sector continues to experience significant digital disruption, with platforms like YooSell joining a growing trend of tech solutions challenging conventional sales methods. The company suggests its platform may particularly appeal to:

  • Cost-conscious sellers looking to reduce estate agency fees
  • Tech-savvy homeowners comfortable managing financial transactions online
  • Sellers wanting greater control over their property listing and viewings

Founder Balram Kumar commented: "With living costs rising, our goal is to help homeowners retain more of their property's value. While technology has transformed most financial transactions, many still feel constrained by traditional property sales methods."

The Future of Property Sales?

YooSell's leadership team draws parallels between their platform and successful online marketplaces in other sectors. Chief Operating Officer Daniel McNeill noted: "We aim to become the AutoTrader equivalent for property. Millions already buy and sell vehicles online comfortably - we believe homeowners deserve similar convenience and transparency when making one of life's most significant financial decisions."

The launch comes as recent industry reports suggest increasing consumer appetite for digital alternatives in property transactions, particularly among younger homeowners. While traditional estate agents still dominate the market, platforms offering greater seller control and reduced fees continue gaining traction.

As the property market evolves, homeowners might wish to research all available options when considering their next move. Digital platforms could potentially offer cost savings, though sellers may need to weigh this against handling more of the sales process themselves.

item.title: Landlord Law Newsround #441
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Recent developments in the UK property market highlight growing concerns around energy efficiency standards, landlord compliance, and tenancy deposit reforms. With key deadlines approaching and regulatory changes on the horizon, both landlords and tenants may need to stay informed about potential impacts on the rental sector.

Fuel Poverty Committee Calls for Landlord Support

The Fuel Poverty Committee has urged the government to direct more support from its £5 billion Warm Homes Fund towards lower-income landlords. With all rental properties required to meet minimum energy efficiency standards by 2030, there are concerns that some landlords may struggle to fund necessary upgrades without targeted assistance.

Experts suggest that without proper incentives, many properties could remain non-compliant, potentially reducing available rental stock and leaving fuel-poor tenants in poorly insulated homes. The committee argues that tailored support could help complete retrofit works that might otherwise be unaffordable for smaller landlords.

Rogue Landlord Crackdown Continues

A landlord has been fined nearly £14,000 and faces inclusion in the national Rogue Landlord Database after failing to address serious safety hazards or obtain mandatory licences. The case highlights councils' increasing use of enforcement powers against non-compliant property owners.

Local authorities have emphasised their commitment to taking appropriate action against landlords who breach housing regulations. The database serves as a public record of serious housing offences, with inclusion potentially affecting a landlord's ability to operate in the rental market.

Potential Overhaul of Tenancy Deposit Schemes

The government has signalled potential reforms to tenancy deposit protection, with Housing Minister Matthew Pennycook suggesting insured schemes (where landlords hold deposits) may be phased out due to concerns about fraud and power imbalances. This could leave custodial schemes, where third parties hold deposits, as the only permitted option.

Industry commentators note that while no final decision has been made, the minister's comments indicate the direction of future policy discussions. Any changes would need to balance tenant protection with maintaining choice for landlords and letting agents.

As the rental market continues to evolve, these developments could have significant implications for both landlords and tenants across England and Wales. Keeping abreast of regulatory changes may help stakeholders navigate an increasingly complex property landscape.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Recent developments in the UK property market highlight growing concerns around energy efficiency standards, landlord compliance, and tenancy deposit reforms. With key deadlines approaching and regulatory changes on the horizon, both landlords and tenants may need to stay informed about potential impacts on the rental sector.

Fuel Poverty Committee Calls for Landlord Support

The Fuel Poverty Committee has urged the government to direct more support from its £5 billion Warm Homes Fund towards lower-income landlords. With all rental properties required to meet minimum energy efficiency standards by 2030, there are concerns that some landlords may struggle to fund necessary upgrades without targeted assistance.

Experts suggest that without proper incentives, many properties could remain non-compliant, potentially reducing available rental stock and leaving fuel-poor tenants in poorly insulated homes. The committee argues that tailored support could help complete retrofit works that might otherwise be unaffordable for smaller landlords.

Rogue Landlord Crackdown Continues

A landlord has been fined nearly £14,000 and faces inclusion in the national Rogue Landlord Database after failing to address serious safety hazards or obtain mandatory licences. The case highlights councils' increasing use of enforcement powers against non-compliant property owners.

Local authorities have emphasised their commitment to taking appropriate action against landlords who breach housing regulations. The database serves as a public record of serious housing offences, with inclusion potentially affecting a landlord's ability to operate in the rental market.

Potential Overhaul of Tenancy Deposit Schemes

The government has signalled potential reforms to tenancy deposit protection, with Housing Minister Matthew Pennycook suggesting insured schemes (where landlords hold deposits) may be phased out due to concerns about fraud and power imbalances. This could leave custodial schemes, where third parties hold deposits, as the only permitted option.

Industry commentators note that while no final decision has been made, the minister's comments indicate the direction of future policy discussions. Any changes would need to balance tenant protection with maintaining choice for landlords and letting agents.

As the rental market continues to evolve, these developments could have significant implications for both landlords and tenants across England and Wales. Keeping abreast of regulatory changes may help stakeholders navigate an increasingly complex property landscape.

item.title: The great property lunch-off returns
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Following their recent triumph at the Agents Giving Dragon Boat Race, the Boys Club is rallying property professionals to participate in the second Property Industry Lunch Break on 26th June. This informal initiative encourages estate agents and industry colleagues to take a proper midday pause between 12pm and 2pm, whether networking with peers or simply recharging away from work pressures.

A Growing Movement for Work-Life Balance

The campaign builds on the success of March's inaugural event, which saw strong participation across the sector. Unlike formal networking functions, there are no registrations or organised activities – just a shared commitment to stepping away from screens and fostering genuine connections. The Boys Club, now boasting over 400 members, originally formed as a support network for agents navigating industry challenges.

Founder Ben Madden reflects: "Our Dragon Boat Race victory proved how virtual connections translate into real teamwork. Many crew members met for the first time that day, yet they rowed to victory together. That spontaneous camaraderie perfectly illustrates why we're bringing back the lunch break initiative."

Why Disconnecting Matters

Industry professionals often face demanding workloads and client pressures, making proper breaks increasingly rare. The Boys Club emphasises that meaningful connections aren't just pleasant – they're fundamental to professional wellbeing and performance.

  • No formal organisation required – simply block out diary time
  • Connect with colleagues, clients or industry contacts
  • Share experiences using #PropertyIndustryLunchBreak

Madden notes: "March's turnout revealed how desperately people needed permission to pause. This isn't about creating another event – it's about reclaiming something we've lost in our always-on culture."

With the summer property market typically busy, the 26th June initiative offers a timely reminder about sustainable working practices. Whether agents choose a working lunch or complete downtime, the movement highlights the value of human connection in an increasingly digital industry.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Following their recent triumph at the Agents Giving Dragon Boat Race, the Boys Club is rallying property professionals to participate in the second Property Industry Lunch Break on 26th June. This informal initiative encourages estate agents and industry colleagues to take a proper midday pause between 12pm and 2pm, whether networking with peers or simply recharging away from work pressures.

A Growing Movement for Work-Life Balance

The campaign builds on the success of March's inaugural event, which saw strong participation across the sector. Unlike formal networking functions, there are no registrations or organised activities – just a shared commitment to stepping away from screens and fostering genuine connections. The Boys Club, now boasting over 400 members, originally formed as a support network for agents navigating industry challenges.

Founder Ben Madden reflects: "Our Dragon Boat Race victory proved how virtual connections translate into real teamwork. Many crew members met for the first time that day, yet they rowed to victory together. That spontaneous camaraderie perfectly illustrates why we're bringing back the lunch break initiative."

Why Disconnecting Matters

Industry professionals often face demanding workloads and client pressures, making proper breaks increasingly rare. The Boys Club emphasises that meaningful connections aren't just pleasant – they're fundamental to professional wellbeing and performance.

  • No formal organisation required – simply block out diary time
  • Connect with colleagues, clients or industry contacts
  • Share experiences using #PropertyIndustryLunchBreak

Madden notes: "March's turnout revealed how desperately people needed permission to pause. This isn't about creating another event – it's about reclaiming something we've lost in our always-on culture."

With the summer property market typically busy, the 26th June initiative offers a timely reminder about sustainable working practices. Whether agents choose a working lunch or complete downtime, the movement highlights the value of human connection in an increasingly digital industry.

item.title: Property industry reacts to biggest homebuying shake-up in years
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The UK government has unveiled a series of proposed reforms to the homebuying process, marking one of the most significant shake-ups in years. The changes aim to streamline property transactions, improve transparency and reduce the number of sales that fall through. With an estimated one in three agreed sales currently failing to complete, the reforms could potentially shorten the average homebuying process by four weeks.

Key Changes to the Homebuying Process

The proposed reforms include several measures designed to modernise the property market. Sellers and estate agents will be required to provide more detailed information upfront, ensuring buyers have access to essential data earlier in the process. This could lead to fewer surprises and delays later on. Additionally, the introduction of earlier binding agreements aims to create greater certainty for both buyers and sellers.

Another notable change is the potential introduction of mandatory qualifications for estate agents. This move is intended to raise professional standards across the sector, ensuring that agents are better equipped to guide clients through the complexities of buying and selling property. These reforms collectively aim to reduce transaction times, minimise fall-throughs and improve the overall experience for home movers.

Industry Reaction to the Reforms

The property industry has welcomed the proposed changes, though some have emphasised the importance of careful implementation. Ben Robinson, managing director of Landmark Estate Agency Services, described the reforms as a "significant change but also a real opportunity." He highlighted the shift from prioritising speed to ensuring properties are marketed properly with accurate information from the outset. Robinson believes this could lead to more informed decisions, faster transactions and a higher completion rate.

Paula Higgins, CEO of the HomeOwners Alliance, also expressed support for the reforms, noting that buying a home remains one of life’s most stressful experiences. She praised the government’s ambition to bring the process into the 21st century but stressed the need for swift action. Higgins emphasised that providing more upfront information, raising standards and adopting digital solutions could reduce delays, costs and frustration for consumers.

While the proposed reforms have been broadly welcomed, their success will depend on how effectively they are implemented. Borrowers and sellers alike may wish to stay informed as these changes unfold, as they could reshape the homebuying process for the better.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

The UK government has unveiled a series of proposed reforms to the homebuying process, marking one of the most significant shake-ups in years. The changes aim to streamline property transactions, improve transparency and reduce the number of sales that fall through. With an estimated one in three agreed sales currently failing to complete, the reforms could potentially shorten the average homebuying process by four weeks.

Key Changes to the Homebuying Process

The proposed reforms include several measures designed to modernise the property market. Sellers and estate agents will be required to provide more detailed information upfront, ensuring buyers have access to essential data earlier in the process. This could lead to fewer surprises and delays later on. Additionally, the introduction of earlier binding agreements aims to create greater certainty for both buyers and sellers.

Another notable change is the potential introduction of mandatory qualifications for estate agents. This move is intended to raise professional standards across the sector, ensuring that agents are better equipped to guide clients through the complexities of buying and selling property. These reforms collectively aim to reduce transaction times, minimise fall-throughs and improve the overall experience for home movers.

Industry Reaction to the Reforms

The property industry has welcomed the proposed changes, though some have emphasised the importance of careful implementation. Ben Robinson, managing director of Landmark Estate Agency Services, described the reforms as a "significant change but also a real opportunity." He highlighted the shift from prioritising speed to ensuring properties are marketed properly with accurate information from the outset. Robinson believes this could lead to more informed decisions, faster transactions and a higher completion rate.

Paula Higgins, CEO of the HomeOwners Alliance, also expressed support for the reforms, noting that buying a home remains one of life’s most stressful experiences. She praised the government’s ambition to bring the process into the 21st century but stressed the need for swift action. Higgins emphasised that providing more upfront information, raising standards and adopting digital solutions could reduce delays, costs and frustration for consumers.

While the proposed reforms have been broadly welcomed, their success will depend on how effectively they are implemented. Borrowers and sellers alike may wish to stay informed as these changes unfold, as they could reshape the homebuying process for the better.

item.title: House prices up, sales down sharply: Is the housing market running out of steam?
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The UK housing market has shown mixed signals in recent weeks, with house prices continuing to rise whilst sales volumes have dipped significantly. Data from the week ending 14th June 2026 reveals a complex picture, with new listings remaining robust but agreed sales falling notably compared to previous years. This trend has led some industry observers to question whether the market is losing momentum.

Listings Remain Strong, but Sales Slow Down

New property listings have remained relatively stable, with 36,500 homes added to the market during the week ending 14th June. This figure is slightly below the previous week’s total of 38,900 but remains above the 10-year average for the same period. Year-to-date, listings are 14% higher than the pre-pandemic average, indicating sustained seller activity. However, the number of homes sold subject to contract (STC) tells a different story. Just 25,100 properties were sold STC during the same week, marking a 10.7% decline nationally compared to the same period in 2025. London saw a sharper drop, with sales down by 14%, whilst regions like the North East experienced only a marginal 1% decrease.

Price Reductions and Withdrawals on the Rise

Price reductions have become more common, with 27,100 properties seeing their asking prices lowered during the week. This equates to 13.4% of all homes on the market in May 2026, slightly above the 2025 average of 12.8%. Additionally, withdrawals have increased, with 64,000 properties removed from the market in May alone. Of these, 45.5% were unsold, suggesting that some sellers are struggling to achieve their desired prices. The gap between initial asking prices and the final agreed sale prices has remained steady at around 16%, consistent with the long-term average.

Net Sales and Exchanges Show Modest Growth

Net sales, which account for properties that have successfully completed the sales process after fall-throughs, totalled 19,200 for the week. This is slightly below the 10-year average but remains in line with the weekly average for 2026. Year-to-date, net sales are 12.5% higher than in 2023 and 6.6% above the 2017–2019 average, indicating some resilience in the market. Exchanges also reflect this trend, with 76,600 properties completing the process in May 2026. Whilst this figure is 5.5% lower than the same period in 2025, it is expected to rise as more transactions are finalised throughout June.

Overall, the UK housing market appears to be navigating a period of adjustment, with strong seller activity contrasting with slower buyer demand. Homeowners may wish to carefully consider market conditions when planning their next move, whilst borrowers might explore competitive mortgage options to capitalise on current trends.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The UK housing market has shown mixed signals in recent weeks, with house prices continuing to rise whilst sales volumes have dipped significantly. Data from the week ending 14th June 2026 reveals a complex picture, with new listings remaining robust but agreed sales falling notably compared to previous years. This trend has led some industry observers to question whether the market is losing momentum.

Listings Remain Strong, but Sales Slow Down

New property listings have remained relatively stable, with 36,500 homes added to the market during the week ending 14th June. This figure is slightly below the previous week’s total of 38,900 but remains above the 10-year average for the same period. Year-to-date, listings are 14% higher than the pre-pandemic average, indicating sustained seller activity. However, the number of homes sold subject to contract (STC) tells a different story. Just 25,100 properties were sold STC during the same week, marking a 10.7% decline nationally compared to the same period in 2025. London saw a sharper drop, with sales down by 14%, whilst regions like the North East experienced only a marginal 1% decrease.

Price Reductions and Withdrawals on the Rise

Price reductions have become more common, with 27,100 properties seeing their asking prices lowered during the week. This equates to 13.4% of all homes on the market in May 2026, slightly above the 2025 average of 12.8%. Additionally, withdrawals have increased, with 64,000 properties removed from the market in May alone. Of these, 45.5% were unsold, suggesting that some sellers are struggling to achieve their desired prices. The gap between initial asking prices and the final agreed sale prices has remained steady at around 16%, consistent with the long-term average.

Net Sales and Exchanges Show Modest Growth

Net sales, which account for properties that have successfully completed the sales process after fall-throughs, totalled 19,200 for the week. This is slightly below the 10-year average but remains in line with the weekly average for 2026. Year-to-date, net sales are 12.5% higher than in 2023 and 6.6% above the 2017–2019 average, indicating some resilience in the market. Exchanges also reflect this trend, with 76,600 properties completing the process in May 2026. Whilst this figure is 5.5% lower than the same period in 2025, it is expected to rise as more transactions are finalised throughout June.

Overall, the UK housing market appears to be navigating a period of adjustment, with strong seller activity contrasting with slower buyer demand. Homeowners may wish to carefully consider market conditions when planning their next move, whilst borrowers might explore competitive mortgage options to capitalise on current trends.

item.title: Landlords and agents face ban on holding tenant deposits
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

The government has announced plans to overhaul tenancy deposit rules, potentially banning landlords and letting agents from holding tenants’ deposits in their own accounts. Under the proposed reforms, all deposits would need to be placed in custodial schemes managed by approved protection providers, marking a significant shift in how rental deposits are handled in England and Wales.

What the changes could mean for tenants and landlords

Currently, landlords and agents can choose between two types of deposit protection schemes: custodial (where funds are held by a third party) and insured (where the landlord holds the deposit but pays for protection). The reforms would eliminate the insured option entirely, requiring all deposits to be held by approved custodial providers.

Housing minister Matthew Pennycook argues this change would create a fairer system by removing what he describes as an "inherent power imbalance" in the current arrangements. Evidence suggests tenants with deposits in custodial schemes are more likely to dispute unfair deductions at the end of tenancies. The government also cites reduced fraud risk as a key benefit, following reports of misuse within the insured scheme model.

Industry reaction and implementation timeline

Property experts note that while the reforms are presented as simplification, they primarily change when obligations arise rather than altering fundamental landlord responsibilities. Kristine Ng of Morr & Co observes that the proposals form part of broader rental sector reforms that began with the Renters’ Rights Act.

For letting agents and landlords, the changes would require adjustments to deposit handling processes that have been in place since tenancy deposit protection rules were introduced in 2007. The move could particularly affect agents who currently earn interest on held deposits.

  • All deposits would move to custodial protection schemes
  • Landlords would lose the option to hold deposits directly
  • Tenants may find it easier to challenge unfair deductions
  • Implementation details remain to be confirmed

While the government has confirmed its intention to proceed with these changes, the exact timeline remains unclear. Further details are expected as ministers progress their wider rental reform programme, which has already introduced measures including the abolition of Section 21 evictions.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

The government has announced plans to overhaul tenancy deposit rules, potentially banning landlords and letting agents from holding tenants’ deposits in their own accounts. Under the proposed reforms, all deposits would need to be placed in custodial schemes managed by approved protection providers, marking a significant shift in how rental deposits are handled in England and Wales.

What the changes could mean for tenants and landlords

Currently, landlords and agents can choose between two types of deposit protection schemes: custodial (where funds are held by a third party) and insured (where the landlord holds the deposit but pays for protection). The reforms would eliminate the insured option entirely, requiring all deposits to be held by approved custodial providers.

Housing minister Matthew Pennycook argues this change would create a fairer system by removing what he describes as an "inherent power imbalance" in the current arrangements. Evidence suggests tenants with deposits in custodial schemes are more likely to dispute unfair deductions at the end of tenancies. The government also cites reduced fraud risk as a key benefit, following reports of misuse within the insured scheme model.

Industry reaction and implementation timeline

Property experts note that while the reforms are presented as simplification, they primarily change when obligations arise rather than altering fundamental landlord responsibilities. Kristine Ng of Morr & Co observes that the proposals form part of broader rental sector reforms that began with the Renters’ Rights Act.

For letting agents and landlords, the changes would require adjustments to deposit handling processes that have been in place since tenancy deposit protection rules were introduced in 2007. The move could particularly affect agents who currently earn interest on held deposits.

  • All deposits would move to custodial protection schemes
  • Landlords would lose the option to hold deposits directly
  • Tenants may find it easier to challenge unfair deductions
  • Implementation details remain to be confirmed

While the government has confirmed its intention to proceed with these changes, the exact timeline remains unclear. Further details are expected as ministers progress their wider rental reform programme, which has already introduced measures including the abolition of Section 21 evictions.

item.title: Property deals stuck in slow lane despite signs of improvement
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

New data reveals modest improvements in UK property transaction times, though nearly half of all sales still take over four months to reach exchange. While May saw a reduction in delays compared to April, industry experts warn that prolonged completion periods continue to pose risks for buyers and sellers across the housing market.

Transaction times show slight improvement

Recent analysis indicates the median time to exchange contracts fell from 127 days in April to 118 days in May. The proportion of transactions exceeding 120 days also dropped from 53.1% to 49.1%. However, these figures suggest almost one in two property sales remains vulnerable to delays and potential collapse.

The statistics highlight ongoing challenges in the conveyancing process, with only 7.7% of transactions completing within six weeks. Meanwhile, the fall-through rate stands at 23.1%, underlining the fragility of many property deals. Industry observers note that while some pressure may be easing, lengthy transaction times continue to hinder market fluidity.

  • Median time to exchange: 118 days (down from 127 in April)
  • Mean completion time: 130 days
  • Fall-through rate: 23.1%
  • Transactions completing within six weeks: 7.7%

Preparation key to smoother transactions

Property professionals emphasise that thorough preparation before marketing a home can significantly improve transaction success rates. Keith Gould of HomeSalesReady notes: "We're seeing a direct correlation between sellers who come to market prepared and transactions that progress more smoothly."

Experts suggest homeowners considering a sale might benefit from having key documentation ready, including tenure papers and material information. A pre-sale survey could also help identify potential issues early in the process. Gould adds: "Upfront preparation isn't a nice-to-have. It's what keeps deals alive."

While May's figures show tentative progress, the data suggests the property market still faces significant challenges with transaction timelines. Borrowers and sellers may wish to factor in potential delays when planning their next move, particularly as nearly half of all sales continue to exceed the four-month mark where fall-through risks increase.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

New data reveals modest improvements in UK property transaction times, though nearly half of all sales still take over four months to reach exchange. While May saw a reduction in delays compared to April, industry experts warn that prolonged completion periods continue to pose risks for buyers and sellers across the housing market.

Transaction times show slight improvement

Recent analysis indicates the median time to exchange contracts fell from 127 days in April to 118 days in May. The proportion of transactions exceeding 120 days also dropped from 53.1% to 49.1%. However, these figures suggest almost one in two property sales remains vulnerable to delays and potential collapse.

The statistics highlight ongoing challenges in the conveyancing process, with only 7.7% of transactions completing within six weeks. Meanwhile, the fall-through rate stands at 23.1%, underlining the fragility of many property deals. Industry observers note that while some pressure may be easing, lengthy transaction times continue to hinder market fluidity.

  • Median time to exchange: 118 days (down from 127 in April)
  • Mean completion time: 130 days
  • Fall-through rate: 23.1%
  • Transactions completing within six weeks: 7.7%

Preparation key to smoother transactions

Property professionals emphasise that thorough preparation before marketing a home can significantly improve transaction success rates. Keith Gould of HomeSalesReady notes: "We're seeing a direct correlation between sellers who come to market prepared and transactions that progress more smoothly."

Experts suggest homeowners considering a sale might benefit from having key documentation ready, including tenure papers and material information. A pre-sale survey could also help identify potential issues early in the process. Gould adds: "Upfront preparation isn't a nice-to-have. It's what keeps deals alive."

While May's figures show tentative progress, the data suggests the property market still faces significant challenges with transaction timelines. Borrowers and sellers may wish to factor in potential delays when planning their next move, particularly as nearly half of all sales continue to exceed the four-month mark where fall-through risks increase.

item.title: Valuation leads jump for agents, says Rightmove
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Estate agents across the UK are seeing a notable surge in property valuation requests, according to the latest data from Rightmove. The property portal reports a 55% increase in valuation leads during the first five months of 2024 compared to the same period last year, signalling renewed activity in the housing market.

Digital tools driving valuation enquiries

Rightmove attributes this significant rise to its recently launched online valuation service and AI-powered enhancements. These innovations allow homeowners to initiate the valuation process digitally before progressing to in-person appointments. The platform’s upgraded tools also help agents prioritise and respond to enquiries more efficiently, potentially reducing response times for motivated sellers.

David Anderson, Rightmove’s director, commented: “Today’s sellers increasingly begin their journey online but still value early contact with local experts. Our digital valuation product bridges this gap, creating quicker, more personal connections between agents and potential vendors.”

Changing seller behaviour in the digital age

The data suggests a shift in how homeowners approach property sales, with many now preferring to research valuations online before committing to traditional valuation appointments. Rightmove’s platform enhancements appear to be meeting this demand by:

  • Streamlining the initial valuation process
  • Helping agents identify the most serious sellers
  • Improving communication channels between agents and vendors

For estate agents, these tools could prove valuable in managing workloads more effectively whilst maintaining personal connections with clients. The ability to filter and prioritise leads might allow agents to focus their efforts where they’re most likely to secure new instructions.

This surge in valuation activity could indicate growing seller confidence in the current market. Homeowners considering a sale might find it helpful to research local market conditions online before contacting multiple agents for valuations, potentially making the selling process more efficient from the outset.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Estate agents across the UK are seeing a notable surge in property valuation requests, according to the latest data from Rightmove. The property portal reports a 55% increase in valuation leads during the first five months of 2024 compared to the same period last year, signalling renewed activity in the housing market.

Digital tools driving valuation enquiries

Rightmove attributes this significant rise to its recently launched online valuation service and AI-powered enhancements. These innovations allow homeowners to initiate the valuation process digitally before progressing to in-person appointments. The platform’s upgraded tools also help agents prioritise and respond to enquiries more efficiently, potentially reducing response times for motivated sellers.

David Anderson, Rightmove’s director, commented: “Today’s sellers increasingly begin their journey online but still value early contact with local experts. Our digital valuation product bridges this gap, creating quicker, more personal connections between agents and potential vendors.”

Changing seller behaviour in the digital age

The data suggests a shift in how homeowners approach property sales, with many now preferring to research valuations online before committing to traditional valuation appointments. Rightmove’s platform enhancements appear to be meeting this demand by:

  • Streamlining the initial valuation process
  • Helping agents identify the most serious sellers
  • Improving communication channels between agents and vendors

For estate agents, these tools could prove valuable in managing workloads more effectively whilst maintaining personal connections with clients. The ability to filter and prioritise leads might allow agents to focus their efforts where they’re most likely to secure new instructions.

This surge in valuation activity could indicate growing seller confidence in the current market. Homeowners considering a sale might find it helpful to research local market conditions online before contacting multiple agents for valuations, potentially making the selling process more efficient from the outset.

item.title: The Renters’ Rights Act’s Quiet Second Act: Why 2026 Turns the Checkout Into a Compliance Record
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The Renters' Reform Act has already transformed the private rental sector with its headline changes in 2026, including the abolition of Section 21 evictions and new restrictions on rent increases. However, many landlords and tenants may not yet have noticed the quieter but equally significant compliance requirements now affecting the end-of-tenancy process.

How Checkout Reports Became Legal Documents

Whilst much attention focused on the initial reforms, the legislation introduced meticulous record-keeping obligations that take full effect this year. Inventory clerks and letting agents now face stricter requirements when compiling checkout reports, which have effectively become compliance documents rather than simple condition assessments.

Key changes include:

  • Mandatory photographic evidence with timestamp and geolocation data
  • Standardised damage classification scales replacing subjective descriptions
  • Full disclosure of all deposit deductions with itemised cost breakdowns
  • Digital submission to a central government portal within seven working days

What This Means for Landlords and Tenants

For landlords, these changes create additional administrative responsibilities. Many are choosing to work with accredited inventory clerks to ensure full compliance, as failure to meet the new standards could jeopardise deposit disputes. The requirement for digital submission also means landlords need to maintain proper records throughout the tenancy.

Tenants benefit from greater transparency in the deposit protection process, but may need to be more diligent when:

  • Documenting the property's condition at move-in
  • Requesting copies of all check-in and checkout reports
  • Challenging any deductions that don't meet the new evidential standards

As the rental market adapts to these changes, both landlords and tenants may wish to familiarise themselves with the updated processes to ensure smooth tenancy transitions and avoid unnecessary disputes.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

The Renters' Reform Act has already transformed the private rental sector with its headline changes in 2026, including the abolition of Section 21 evictions and new restrictions on rent increases. However, many landlords and tenants may not yet have noticed the quieter but equally significant compliance requirements now affecting the end-of-tenancy process.

How Checkout Reports Became Legal Documents

Whilst much attention focused on the initial reforms, the legislation introduced meticulous record-keeping obligations that take full effect this year. Inventory clerks and letting agents now face stricter requirements when compiling checkout reports, which have effectively become compliance documents rather than simple condition assessments.

Key changes include:

  • Mandatory photographic evidence with timestamp and geolocation data
  • Standardised damage classification scales replacing subjective descriptions
  • Full disclosure of all deposit deductions with itemised cost breakdowns
  • Digital submission to a central government portal within seven working days

What This Means for Landlords and Tenants

For landlords, these changes create additional administrative responsibilities. Many are choosing to work with accredited inventory clerks to ensure full compliance, as failure to meet the new standards could jeopardise deposit disputes. The requirement for digital submission also means landlords need to maintain proper records throughout the tenancy.

Tenants benefit from greater transparency in the deposit protection process, but may need to be more diligent when:

  • Documenting the property's condition at move-in
  • Requesting copies of all check-in and checkout reports
  • Challenging any deductions that don't meet the new evidential standards

As the rental market adapts to these changes, both landlords and tenants may wish to familiarise themselves with the updated processes to ensure smooth tenancy transitions and avoid unnecessary disputes.
